era_context: Mid-3rd century BCE. The Hellenistic world was dominated by the Successor States. The Second Syrian War was ongoing. Ashoka's Mauryan Empire was at its height. The First Punic War between Rome and Carthage was in its eighth year. China remained in the Warring States period.

events.title: First Punic War: Battle of Ecnomus
events.description: In 256 BCE, the Roman fleet under the command of Marcus Atilius Regulus and Lucius Manlius Vulso achieved a major naval victory against the Carthaginian fleet at Ecnomus (modern Licata) in Sicily. This was one of the largest naval battles of antiquity, with both sides deploying hundreds of ships. The Roman victory was decisive and demonstrated Rome's naval superiority. Following this victory, Regulus invaded North Africa, bringing the war to Carthage's homeland. This campaign would ultimately fail, but the Battle of Ecnomus marked a turning point in Roman naval dominance.
key_figures: Marcus Atilius Regulus (Roman consul and admiral)
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Ecnomus is well-attested in Roman historiography. The date 256 BCE is scholarly consensus. The battle's significance and scale are confirmed by multiple sources.

events.title: Ashoka's Reign at Its Height
events.description: By 256 BCE, Ashoka's reign was at its zenith. The Mauryan Empire under his rule extended across most of the Indian subcontinent, from the Deccan to the Himalayan foothills. Ashoka's administrative reforms, based on Dhamma (Buddhist ethics), had transformed the empire's governance. His edicts were inscribed across the empire in multiple languages and scripts. His missionary activities were spreading Buddhism beyond India. Ashoka's reign represents a high point of ancient Indian civilization and the height of Mauryan power.
events.certainty_note: Ashoka's edicts are contemporary epigraphic evidence confirming his reign and influence. The dating to 256 BCE is approximate based on regnal chronology.

events.title: Second Syrian War: Ongoing Conflict
events.region: Eastern Mediterranean, Seleucid Empire, Ptolemaic Egypt
events.description: The Second Syrian War (260–253 BCE) continued in 256 BCE with military operations in the eastern Mediterranean. Both the Seleucid Empire and Ptolemaic Egypt were engaged in campaigns for control of coastal territories and trade routes. The conflict would eventually be resolved through a marriage alliance between Antiochus II and Berenice II, but in 256 BCE, active warfare was ongoing.
events.certainty_note: The Second Syrian War is well-attested in Hellenistic historiography, but specific events in 256 BCE are not independently documented.

events.title: Qin-Zhao Conflict in the Warring States Period
events.description: In 256 BCE, the Qin state under King Zhao was engaged in military campaigns against the Zhao state and other rivals. The Warring States period was characterized by constant warfare and shifting alliances. Qin's legalist administrative system and military innovations were giving it an advantage. The specific events of 256 BCE are not well-documented, but the broader context of Qin expansion is clear.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled c. 109 BCE

events.certainty_note: Qin's expansion during this period is attested in Chinese historiography, but specific events in 256 BCE are not independently confirmed.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Ecnomus is one of the best-attested naval battles of antiquity, but some scholars debate the exact number of ships involved and the precise location. The Second Syrian War's specific events in 256 BCE are not independently documented. Qin's military campaigns in 256 BCE are not confirmed in contemporary sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 256 BCE is similar to previous years: uneven and heavily dependent on later compilations. The Battle of Ecnomus is well-attested in Roman historiography, making 256 BCE one of the better-documented years in this period. Ashoka's edicts provide contemporary evidence from South Asia. Chinese history relies entirely on the Shiji, compiled 150+ years after events.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Ecnomus enabled Regulus's invasion of North Africa in 255 BCE
Ashoka's reign continued until approximately 232 BCE
